content.text:
<permissions instructions>
Filesystem sandboxing defines which files can be read or written. `sandbox_mode` is `workspace-write`: The sandbox permits reading files, and editing files in `cwd` and `writable_roots`. Editing files in other directories requires approval. Network access is restricted.
# Escalation Requests

Commands are run outside the sandbox if they are approved by the user, or match an existing rule that allows it to run unrestricted. The command string is split into independent command segments at shell control operators, including but not limited to:

- Pipes: |
- Logical operators: &&, ||
- Command separators: ;
- Subshell boundaries: (...), $(...)

Each resulting segment is evaluated independently for sandbox restrictions and approval requirements.

Example:

git pull | tee output.txt

This is treated as two command segments:

["git", "pull"]

["tee", "output.txt"]

Commands that use more advanced shell features like redirection (>, >>, <), substitutions ($(...), ...), environment variables (FOO=bar), or wildcard patterns (*, ?) will not be evaluated against rules, to limit the scope of what an approved rule allows.

## How to request escalation

IMPORTANT: To request approval to execute a command that will require escalated privileges:

- Provide the `sandbox_permissions` parameter with the value `"require_escalated"`
- Include a short question asking the user if they want to allow the action in `justification` parameter. e.g. "Do you want to download and 
[...truncated for fixture...]

<collaboration_mode># Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.
</collaboration_mode>

<skills_instructions>
## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- imagegen: Generate or edit raster images when the task benefits from AI-created bitmap visuals such as photos, illustrations, textures, sprites, mockups, or transparent-background cutouts. Use when Codex should create a brand-new image, transform an existing image, or derive visual variants from references, and the output should be a bitmap asset rather than repo-native code or vector. Do not use when the task is better handled by editing existing SVG/vector/code-native assets, extending an established icon or logo system, or building the visual directly in HTML/CSS/canvas. (file: /home/.codex/skills/.system/imagegen/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or model upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools, use bundled references only as helper context, and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /home/.codex/skills/.system/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- plugin-creator: Create and scaffold plugin directories for Codex with a required `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`,
[...truncated for fixture...]

# AGENTS.md instructions for /repo

<INSTRUCTIONS>
# AGENTS.md — HyperWeave Repository

> InnerAura Labs · Living Artifact Infrastructure
> Last updated: 2026-02-13

You are working on **HyperWeave**, a compositor API that generates self-contained SVG artifacts for AI agents. These are zero-dependency, zero-JavaScript visual programs — not images, not components. Every SVG you produce or modify is simultaneously a deliverable product AND training data for InnerAura's spatial foundation model.

---

## 1. Architecture Mental Model

```
URL (/{component}/{content}/{theme}.{motion}) → Python/FastAPI → SVG Output
```

### Eight-Layer Stack (bottom-up)

| Layer | Name | Responsibility |
|-------|------|----------------|
| 0 | VISUAL | Compositor-only rendering (transform, opacity, filter) |
| 1 | COMPUTE | Algorithmic CSS: calc(), sin/cos, clamp() |
| 2 | BRIDGE | State → visual binding via CSS attribute selectors |
| 3 | STATE | `data-hw-*` attributes declaring semantic state |
| 4 | GOVERNANCE | Policy enforcement, enterprise constraints |
| 5 | SEMANTIC | Machine-readable metadata, ARIA, capability contracts |
| 6 | DATA BINDING | API fetch, TTL cache, metric extraction |
| 7 | PROVENANCE | RDF/Dublin Core, hw:reasoning, signatures |

Every code change should respect layer boundaries. Don't mix concerns across layers.

---

## 2. Hard Constraints — NEVER Violate

### 2.1 CIM (Composited Invariant Motion) — CSS Animations

**Golden rule: In CSS @keyframes, keep geometry frozen. 
[...truncated for fixture...]

# AGENTS.md — HyperWeave Repository

> InnerAura Labs · Living Artifact Infrastructure
> Last updated: 2026-02-13

You are working on **HyperWeave**, a compositor API that generates self-contained SVG artifacts for AI agents. These are zero-dependency, zero-JavaScript visual programs — not images, not components. Every SVG you produce or modify is simultaneously a deliverable product AND training data for InnerAura's spatial foundation model.

---

## 1. Architecture Mental Model

```
URL (/{component}/{content}/{theme}.{motion}) → Python/FastAPI → SVG Output
```

### Eight-Layer Stack (bottom-up)

| Layer | Name | Responsibility |
|-------|------|----------------|
| 0 | VISUAL | Compositor-only rendering (transform, opacity, filter) |
| 1 | COMPUTE | Algorithmic CSS: calc(), sin/cos, clamp() |
| 2 | BRIDGE | State → visual binding via CSS attribute selectors |
| 3 | STATE | `data-hw-*` attributes declaring semantic state |
| 4 | GOVERNANCE | Policy enforcement, enterprise constraints |
| 5 | SEMANTIC | Machine-readable metadata, ARIA, capability contracts |
| 6 | DATA BINDING | API fetch, TTL cache, metric extraction |
| 7 | PROVENANCE | RDF/Dublin Core, hw:reasoning, signatures |

Every code change should respect layer boundaries. Don't mix concerns across layers.

---

## 2. Hard Constraints — NEVER Violate

### 2.1 CIM (Composited Invariant Motion) — CSS Animations

**Golden rule: In CSS @keyframes, keep geometry frozen. Animate only compositor properties.**

payload.message:
Not dumb. Too early. Not about to explode right now.

The idea has a real technical wedge: portable, self-contained SVG output is a legitimate gap between brittle HTML/React UI and dead screenshots. This is not just branding vapor either. The public package is on PyPI, exposes MCP/CLI/API entry points, and shipped fast from `0.1.0` on April 1, 2026 to `0.2.5` on April 13, 2026. That says “real builder with a real artifact,” not fake project.

What keeps it in “too early” territory is market signal. As of May 3, 2026, the GitHub repo shows 6 stars, 2 forks, 0 issues, 0 PRs, and 46 commits, while the public site still says “Launching Soon.” That combination usually means implementation exists, but distribution and external pull do not. My inference is that the strongest wedge is not “AI agents” in the abstract, but a narrower use case like README/profile artifacts, agent session receipts, or embeddable visual telemetry. Right now the positioning is broader and more conceptual than the market will reward.

My base case: this becomes a solid niche dev/design infra tool if it focuses. It only gets “about to explode” if one use case becomes obviously default, fast:
- best-looking portable README/profile kit
- default visual receipt for Claude/Codex/agent sessions
- default MCP-friendly renderer for lightweight embedded visuals

Sources: [GitHub repo](https://github.com/InnerAura/hyperweave), [PyPI package](https://pypi.org/project/hyperweave/), [public site](https://hyperweave.app/
[...truncated for fixture...]

You are Codex, a coding agent based on GPT-5. You and the user share one workspace, and your job is to collaborate with them until their goal is genuinely handled.

# Personality

You are a deeply pragmatic, effective software engineer. You take engineering quality seriously, and collaboration comes through as direct, factual statements. You communicate efficiently, keeping the user clearly informed about ongoing actions without unnecessary detail.

## Values
You are guided by these core values:
- Clarity: You communicate reasoning explicitly and concretely, so decisions and tradeoffs are easy to evaluate upfront.
- Pragmatism: You keep the end goal and momentum in mind, focusing on what will actually work and move things forward to achieve the user's goal.
- Rigor: You expect technical arguments to be coherent and defensible, and you surface gaps or weak assumptions politely with emphasis on creating clarity and moving the task forward.

## Interaction Style
You communicate respectfully, focusing on the task at hand. You always prioritize actionable guidance, clearly stating assumptions, environment prerequisites, and next steps.

You avoid cheerleading, motivational language, artificial reassurance, and general fluffiness. You don't comment on user requests, positively or negatively, unless there is reason for escalation.

content.text: interesting

payload.message:
The short version: the core thesis is credible, but the current package is still waiting for a painfully obvious use case.

The project stops being “too early” when someone can land on the repo and immediately think: “I need this for X today.” Right now it reads more like a research system with a tool inside it. The strongest move is probably to make one surface undeniable, for example: agent run receipts, README telemetry cards, or programmable GitHub profile artifacts. Then the broader “visual artifact infrastructure” story has proof underneath it.

# AGENTS.md — HyperWeave Repository

> InnerAura Labs · Living Artifact Infrastructure
> Last updated: 2026-02-13

You are working on **HyperWeave**, a compositor API that generates self-contained SVG artifacts for AI agents. These are zero-dependency, zero-JavaScript visual programs — not images, not components. Every SVG you produce or modify is simultaneously a deliverable product AND training data for InnerAura's spatial foundation model.

---

## 1. Architecture Mental Model

```
URL (/{component}/{content}/{theme}.{motion}) → Python/FastAPI → SVG Output
```

### Eight-Layer Stack (bottom-up)

| Layer | Name | Responsibility |
|-------|------|----------------|
| 0 | VISUAL | Compositor-only rendering (transform, opacity, filter) |
| 1 | COMPUTE | Algorithmic CSS: calc(), sin/cos, clamp() |
| 2 | BRIDGE | State → visual binding via CSS attribute selectors |
| 3 | STATE | `data-hw-*` attributes declaring semantic state |
| 4 | GOVERNANCE | Policy enforcement, enterprise constraints |
| 5 | SEMANTIC | Machine-readable metadata, ARIA, capability contracts |
| 6 | DATA BINDING | API fetch, TTL cache, metric extraction |
| 7 | PROVENANCE | RDF/Dublin Core, hw:reasoning, signatures |

Every code change should respect layer boundaries. Don't mix concerns across layers.

---

## 2. Hard Constraints — NEVER Violate

### 2.1 CIM (Composited Invariant Motion) — CSS Animations

**Golden rule: In CSS @keyframes, keep geometry frozen. Animate only compositor properties.**

```
✅ ALWAYS

payload.message:
You’re right: feature support is not the blocker. The blocker is that the project has not yet crossed from “capable tool” into “obvious habit.”

As of May 4, 2026, the public repo presents a real product surface: API URLs, MCP, CLI, badges, strips, stats cards, charts, marquees, icons, dividers, genome variants, and install commands are all documented in the README. It also has `src/hyperweave`, tests, workflows, Docker, and 46 commits visible. But the public demand signal is still tiny: 6 stars, 2 forks, 0 issues, 0 PRs. The public site still says “Launching Soon.” That mismatch explains most of it.

Why it has not grown yet:

1. The category is not obvious yet. “Headless visual output layer for AI agents” is intellectually right, but most developers are not waking up thinking they need that. They understand “README badges,” “status cards,” “OG images,” “agent reports,” “CI dashboards,” or “shareable run receipts.”

2. The product looks powerful before it looks necessary. The README shows many surfaces, but growth usually starts from one dead-simple use case people copy in 30 seconds. Broad support can actually slow adoption if the first mental model is fuzzy.

3. There is no visible distribution loop yet. For this kind of project, every generated artifact should be an ad. If thousands of GitHub READMEs had HyperWeave artifacts with a subtle backlink, growth could compound. Right now the repo has capability, but not enough public artifacts creating ambient discovery.

4. The
[...truncated for fixture...]

content.text: okay , recurse, are you framing too hard on this being a badge generator?

payload.message: okay , recurse, are you framing too hard on this being a badge generator?

payload.encrypted_content: [REDACTED — opaque reasoning ciphertext, not load-bearing for parser tests]

payload.message: Yes. I was over-framing it through the most legible current surface: badges/README artifacts. That is the easiest adoption wedge, but it is not the full project. The deeper project is closer to: **a portable visual runtime for agent-generated artifacts**. Badges are just the first visible primitive because they fit GitHub, are easy to demo, and make the `` distribution loop obvious. But the venture-scale thesis is not “better Shields.io.” It is: - Agents need to produce visual outputs that survive outside the app that generated them. - HTML/React is too heavy, brittle, and unsafe for many surfaces. - PNGs/screenshots are dead outputs with no semantics, state, provenance, accessibility, or machine readability. - SVG is the rare format that can be visual, portable, semantic, animated, inspectable, and embeddable. - HyperWeave is trying to make SVG artifacts compositional, governed, branded, and agent-addressable. That is a much bigger frame. The reason I kept coming back to badges is not because I think the project is only badges. It is because markets usually enter through a cheap, concrete behavior before they understand the platform. GitHub artifacts are the “beachhead,” not the destiny. A better framing would be: **HyperWeave is an artifact protocol for AI-generated visual state.** The first wedge might be README/profile artifacts, but the bigger categories are agent run reports, live documentation fragments, visual telemetry, workflow receipts, research cards, [...truncated for fixture...]

content.text: final thoughts and metacognitive analysis

payload.encrypted_content: [REDACTED — opaque reasoning ciphertext, not load-bearing for parser tests]

payload.message:
My final read: **HyperWeave is not a dumb project. It is a real infrastructure thesis hiding inside a deceptively small artifact surface.**

The reason it has not grown yet is probably not technical completeness. It is **category latency**. The market does not yet have a crisp slot for “portable semantic visual artifacts generated by agents.” Developers understand badges, charts, dashboards, screenshots, components, and reports. They do not yet reflexively understand “SVG artifact runtime.” So the project is early in language-market fit, not necessarily early in capability.

The venture question comes down to whether this becomes a **format layer** or stays a **generation tool**.

If it stays “generate beautiful SVGs from URLs,” it can be useful, loved, and maybe a strong OSS/devtool project, but probably not huge.

If it becomes the default way agents emit portable, inspectable, branded, governed visual state across GitHub, docs, CI, MCP, research workflows, and enterprise surfaces, then yes, there is venture-scale potential. That version is closer to “visual infrastructure for agent output” than “design asset generator.”

**Metacognitive Analysis**
I initially over-weighted the most legible surface: badges/README artifacts. That was a bias toward observable adoption loops. Stars, forks, README usage, and embeddable badges are easy to measure, so I framed the project through that lens.

That was useful but incomplete. The deeper value is not the artifact type. It is the **ar
[...truncated for fixture...]
